content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 25, 2019

Tom Schroeter
City Attorney
254 H Street
Bakersfield, CA 93304

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-113

Dear Mr. Schroeter:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act, and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kern County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

Does Tehachapi City Councilmember and aviation business-owner Kenneth Hetge have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act or Section 1090 that prevents him from taking part in the City Council's consideration of the general fund budget and airport budget, both of which would potentially include funds for repairs to the Tehachapi Municipal Airport? If so, may he speak at City Council meetings as a member of the general public regarding the effects the budget decisions would have on his business?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-113
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Under the Act, Councilmember Hetge is prohibited from taking part in budgetary decisions involving potential airport repairs. As explained in a prior letter, he is also prohibited from directly or indirectly attempting to influence the City Council in the execution of airport repair contracts, which includes speaking as a member of the general public regarding the funding for such contracts. However, Councilmember Hetge may still take part in the final vote to adopt or reject the budget after the separate decisions affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized as permitted by Regulation 18706(c). Councilmember Hetge may also take part in the City Council's budgetary decisions separate from the airport related decisions to the extent that they are properly segmented, as required under Regulation 18706(a).

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

In the recent Schroeter Advice Letter, No. A-19-006, we determined that Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge—who operates a business out of the Tehachapi Municipal Airport—has a disqualifying financial interest under Section 1090 in potential contracts for Tehachapi Municipal Airport repairs, including FAA grants that would help fund repairs. It was noted that the construction of the airport repair project could impact access between the airport's runway and the hangars owned by Councilmember Hetge's business. Ultimately, we determined that although Councilmember Hetge was disqualified from taking part in the contracting process under Section 1090, the "rule of necessity" permitted the City Council to contract for the necessary repairs to ensure that the Airport continued to operate safely.

In light of Schroeter Advice Letter, No. A-19-006, Councilmember Hetge sought additional advice regarding potential actions he could take as a public official disqualified under Section 1090. In Hetge Advice Letter, No. A-19-047, Councilmember Hetge explained he was concerned that decisions being made by the City of Tehachapi regarding infrastructure and policies associated with the airport would impact his business, to the point that he would go out of business. We explained that under Regulation 18704(d)(2), a public official otherwise disqualified from taking part in a governmental decision under the Act may appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function as a member of the general public on matters related solely to his or personal interests, including a business entity owned entirely by him or her, his or her immediate family, or some combination thereof. However, Councilmember Hetge was prohibited from taking part in the airport repair contracting process under Section 1090, which does not contain any exception similar to Regulation 18704. Accordingly, we concluded that he was prohibited from directly or indirectly attempting to influence the City Council in the execution of a contract, including through participation as a "member of the general public" at a City Council meeting.

The Tehachapi City Council began budget hearings in June. Included in the hearings will be the airport budget, which will include the amount of matching funds Tehachapi is required to allocate for construction of the airport repair project pursuant to the requirements of the FAA grant. At the same time, the City's general fund budget will be presented, which will include funds to be allocated to the airport, including funds for construction of the taxiway project. The general fund subsidizes the airport budget. In a follow-up email, you clarified that the 2019-2020 airport budget includes the amount that Tehachapi will have to contribute to the FAA grant for the design phase of the project (estimated by airport engineers). You also explained that Tehachapi approves a five-year

File No. A-19-113
Page No. 3

budget each year. The construction phase of the project is described within the five-year budget. The amount allocated is an estimate given by Techachapi's airport engineers, but the exact amount will ultimately depend on the design that is approved for the project and the bids received when it is bid out. The FAA will pay for 90% of the construction costs and Techachapi will pay the additional 10%. However, Techachapi will also apply to CalTrans for funding 5% of the project costs and, if successful, Techachapi would pay the remaining 5%.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest Provisions

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on certain enumerated economic interests, including "[a]ny business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(a).) Councilmember Hetge presumably has invested more than $2,000 in the business he operates out of the Airport, and therefore has an economic interest in this business.

A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) It is reasonably foreseeable that items pertaining to the Techachapi Municipal Airport, including funds allotted for airport repairs, could have a financial effect on Councilmember Hetge's business entity interest. In the short-run, the airport repairs project could negatively affect the business by impacting hangar access, but in the long-run, the project would ensure that the airport remains safe and operational, which would have a positive impact on airport businesses.

Under Regulation 18702.1(a)(3), as adopted on July 18, 2019, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity's assets and liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000, or five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues, and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000. Likewise, a financial effect on a business entity interest is material if the decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $250,000, or one percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).) In his previous request for advice, Councilmember Hetge indicated his concern that the repair project would impact his business to the point that he might go out of business. Accordingly, it is foreseeable that the decisions will result in

File No. A-19-113
Page No. 4

a decrease of at least five percent of annual gross revenues, and Councilmember Hetge is
disqualified from the decisions. (See Hetge Advice Letter, No. A-19-047.)²

Segmentation

Regulation 18706(c) provides, "[o]nce all the separate decisions related to a budget . . . .
affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final
vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget . . . ." Accordingly, although Councilmember Hetge has
a disqualifying financial interest, he may still take part in the final vote to adopt or reject the budget
after the separate decisions affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized.

Councilmember Hetge is also not inherently disqualified from taking part in budgetary
decisions unrelated to the airport or airport repair project. Regulation 18706(a) provides that an
agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial interest, to allow
participation by the official, provided all of the following conditions apply:

(1)      The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
         into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
         which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

(2)      The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
         the other decisions;

(3)      The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
         and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified
         official's participation in any way; and

(4)      Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
         the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening
         of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was
         disqualified.

For purposes of the above conditions, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of
one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.
(Regulation 18706(b).) Accordingly, if individual budgetary decisions are properly segmented, as
provided under Regulation 18706(a), Councilmember Hetge may take part in the decisions as long
as they would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on his economic interests,
including budget decisions that would affect the airport repair contracting process.

Given our conclusion that Councilmember Hetge is generally prohibited under the Act from
taking part in the budgetary decisions, we need not analyze whether he is also disqualified from
taking part in those decisions under Section 1090. To the extent that the decisions are properly
segmented, Councilmember Hetge would not be disqualified from taking part in budgetary
decisions unrelated to the airport or airport repair project under Section 1090.

_______________

² Note that although Regulation 18702.1 was repealed and adopted on July 18, 2019—after your request for
advice was submitted—Councilmember Hetge also would have been disqualified from taking part in the budgetary
decisions under the prior version of Regulation 18702.1.

File No. A-19-113
Page No. 5

Finally, in regard to the final vote to adopt or reject the budget, the decision would not be a
contract and therefore would not implicate Section 1090. For Section 1090 to apply, the matter at
issue must involve a contract. While, traditional contract principles are applied to determine
whether a contract is involved for purposes of Section 1090, both the Attorney General's Office and
the Commission have previously opined that a regulatory matter that does not involve a "contract"
for purposes of Section 1090. (See 84 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 34 (2001); Carvalho Advice Letter, No.
A-16-265; and Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229.) Like a regulatory matter, we find that the final
budgetary vote broadly applies to the jurisdiction as a whole and is not a "contract" for purposes of
Section 1090.

Participation as Member of the Public

Under Regulation 18704(d)(2), a public official otherwise disqualified from taking part in a
governmental decision under the Act may appear before his or her agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function as a member of the general public on matters related solely to his
personal interests, including a business entity owned entirely by him or her, his or her immediate
family, or some combination thereof. However, in Hetge Advice Letter, No. A-19-047, we advised
Councilmember Hetge, "under Section 1090 [he] may not directly or indirectly attempt to influence
the City Council in the execution of the [airport repair project] contract." That advice remains
applicable. To the extent that an attempt to comment "as a member of the general public," per
Regulation 18704(d)(2), would merely be an indirect attempt to influence the City Council's airport
repair contracting process—e.g., directly attempting to influence the budgetary decisions as a means
of indirectly influencing the airport repair contracting process—such conduct would still be
prohibited.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

Conflict of Interest Provisions 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any state or local public official from making, 
participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in

within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a

thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(a).) Councilmember Hetge presumably has 
invested more than $2,000 in the business he operates out of the Airport, and therefore has an 
economic interest in his business. 
A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if 
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) It is reasonably foreseeable that items pertaining to the 
Tehachapi Municipal Airport, including funds allotted for airport repairs, could have a financial

project would ensure that the airport remains safe and operational, which would have a positive

Under Regulation 18702.l(a)(3), as adopted on July 18, 2019, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is 
material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, 
or the value of the entity's assets and liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000, or 
five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues, and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000. 
Likewise, a financial effect on a business entity interest is material if the decision may cause the 
entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to 
or more than $250,000, or one percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in 
expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.l(a)(4).) In his previous request for advice, 
Councilmember Hetge indicated his concern that the repair project would impact his business to the

a decrease of at least five percent of annual gross revenues, and Councilmember Hetge is 
disqualified from the decisions. (See Hetge Advice Letter, No. A-19-047.)2 
Segmentation 
Regulation: 18706( c) provides, "[ o ]nee all the separate decisions related to a budget .... 
affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final 
vote to adopt or reject the agency's budget .... " Accordingly, although Councilmember Hetge has 
a disqualifying financial interest, he may still take part in the final vote to adopt or reject the budget 
after the separate decisions affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized. 
Councilmember Hetge is also not inherently disqualified from taking part in budgetary 
decisions unrelated to the airport or airport repair project. Regulation l 8706(a) provides that an 
agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial interest, to allow 
participation by the official, provided all of the following conditions apply: 
(1) 
The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) 
The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) 
The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified 
official's participation in any way; and 
( 4) 
Once tht, decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening 
of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified. 
For purposes of the above conditions, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of 
one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. 
(Regulation 18706(b).) Accordingly, if individual budgetary decisions are properly segmented, as

as they would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on his economic interests, 
including budget decisions that would affect the airport repair contracting process.

taking part in the budgetary decisions, we need not analyze whether he is also disqualified from 
taking part in those decisions under Section 1090. To the extent that the decisions are properly 
segmented, Councilmember Hetge would not be disqualified from taking part in budgetary 
decisions unrelated to the airport or airport repair project under Section 1090. 
2 Note that although Regulation 18702.1 was repealed and adopted on July 18, 2019-after your request for 
advice was submitted-Councilmember Hetge also would have been disqualified from taking part in the budgetary 
decisions under the prior version of Regulation 18702.1.

Under Regulation 18704( d)(2), a public official otherwise disqualified from taking part in a 
governmental decision under the Act may appear before his or her agency in the course of its 
prescribed governmental function as a member of the general public on matters related solely to his

disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act or Section I 090 that prevents him from taking part in

at City Council meetings as a member of the general public regarding the effects the budget 
decisions would have on his business?

Under the Act, Councilmember Hetge is prohibited from taking part in budgetary decisions 
involving potential airport repairs. As explained in a prior letter, he is also prohibited from directly 
or indirectly attempting to influence the City Council in the execution of airport repair contracts, 
which includes speaking as a member of the general public regarding the funding for such

by Regulation 18706(c). Councilmember Hetge may also take part in the City Council's budgetary 
decisions separate from the airport related decisions to the extent that they are properly segmented, 
as required under Regulation 18706(a).
